facts.text: Kinderhook’s name, first recorded as “Kinderhoek” in 1614, means “children’s corner” in Dutch and is one of New York’s oldest Dutch‑derived place names.
In 1809 Washington Irving stayed in Kinderhook for eight weeks, writing parts of his history of New York while visiting the town’s Dutch colonial homes.
The Luykas Van Alen House, built around 1737, inspired Irving’s “Van Tassel” family farm in *The Legend of Sleepy Hollow* and is a National Historic Landmark.
The town’s Columbia County Historical Society owns four historic properties, including the 1819 James Vanderpoel House, the 1850 Ichabod Crane Schoolhouse, and the 1915 museum building.
Martin Van Buren’s former home, Lindenwald, sits in the southwestern part of the town and now serves as a National Historic Site.
Kinderhook’s historic center hosts the James Vanderpoel House and the CCHS Museum & Library, while the Luykas Van Alen House and Ichabod Crane Schoolhouse lie just outside the village limits.
